---
title: "Module 1: Measurement"
subtitle: "Discriminant Validity, Omitted Variable Bias, Measurement Invariance, Latent Classes, and Outliers"
---

<iframe src="https://liveeur-my.sharepoint.com/personal/58542dsc_eur_nl/_layouts/15/Doc.aspx?sourcedoc={4a5ede60-44ee-4081-9230-dba63b2c5ecb}&action=embedview&wdAr=1.7777777777777777" width="100%" height="500px" frameborder="0" allowfullscreen title="Module 1 Part 1: Validity"></iframe>

## Overview

Good measurement is the foundation of good research, yet it is the step most often skipped. This module asks: *does your scale actually measure the construct you intend?* — and introduces the tools to find out.

We cover discriminant validity, omitted variable bias from measurement error, measurement invariance across groups, latent class structure, and outlier detection. Each topic is treated both conceptually and with live R code.

---

## Learning Goals

By the end of this module you should be able to:

- Distinguish construct validity from reliability, and explain why reliability alone is insufficient
- Diagnose discriminant validity problems using CFA and AVE/correlation comparisons
- Explain how classical measurement error biases regression coefficients and in which direction
- Test for measurement invariance across groups using configural, metric, and scalar CFA models
- Identify latent class structure using mixture models and interpret class profiles
- Detect and reason about outliers using Cook's D, DFFITS, and leverage diagnostics

---

## Recommended Reading

| Paper | Why it matters |
|---|---|
| Flake & Fried (2020) — *Measurement Schmeasurement* | The best accessible critique of how measurement is treated in psychology and management |
| Borsboom, Mellenbergh & van Heerden (2003) | What does it actually mean for a test to measure a construct? |
| Arkes & Tetlock (2004) | A worked example of construct validity problems in political judgment research |
| Vosgerau et al. (2019) — *Exerting Self-Control is Not Sacrificing Pleasure* | How reframing the DV changes conclusions |

All papers are available in the `Module 1 - Recommended Reading/` folder.

---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = TRUE,
  fig.width = 8,
  fig.height = 5,
  fig.path = "figs/",
  dev = "svg"
)

dir.create("figs", recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)
```

```{=html}
<style>
/* ── Floating bottom control bar ──────────────────────────────────────────── */
#global-output-toggle {
  position: fixed;
  bottom: 0;
  left: 0;
  right: 0;
  z-index: 9999;
  display: flex;
  flex-wrap: wrap;
  gap: 10px;
  justify-content: center;
  align-items: center;
  padding: 11px 24px;
  background: #1e3a5f;
  border-top: 3px solid #4a90d9;
  box-shadow: 0 -3px 16px rgba(0,0,0,0.35);
}
#global-output-toggle span {
  color: #c8d8ed;
  font-size: 0.85em;
  font-weight: 700;
  letter-spacing: 0.6px;
  text-transform: uppercase;
  margin-right: 6px;
}
/* Reserve space at page bottom so content never hides behind the bar */
body { padding-bottom: 70px !important; }

.gtbtn {
  padding: 9px 24px;
  color: #ffffff;
  border-radius: 7px;
  font-size: 1em;
  font-weight: 700;
  cursor: pointer;
  letter-spacing: 0.2px;
  transition: background 0.15s, border-color 0.15s;
  white-space: nowrap;
  border: 2px solid transparent;
}
/* Code button — blue */
.gtbtn.code-btn   { background: #2563a8; border-color: #5b9bd5; }
.gtbtn.code-btn:hover { background: #1a4a80; border-color: #90bfe8; }
/* Output button — teal/green */
.gtbtn.output-btn { background: #2d6a4f; border-color: #52b788; }
.gtbtn.output-btn:hover { background: #1b4332; border-color: #74c69d; }

/* ── Per-output collapsible wrappers ──────────────────────────────────────── */
details.out-fold { margin: 6px 0 14px 0; }
summary.out-fold-btn {
  display: inline-flex;
  align-items: center;
  gap: 7px;
  padding: 8px 20px;
  background: #eef4fb;
  border: 2px solid #4a90d9;
  border-radius: 7px;
  cursor: pointer;
  font-size: 0.94em;
  font-weight: 700;
  color: #1a3a6b;
  user-select: none;
  list-style: none;
  transition: background 0.12s;
}
summary.out-fold-btn::-webkit-details-marker { display: none; }
summary.out-fold-btn:hover { background: #cfe2f7; }
details[open].out-fold > summary.out-fold-btn { background: #d6ecff; }
summary.out-fold-btn .fold-icon { font-size: 1em; }
details[open] .fold-icon-closed { display: none; }
details:not([open]) .fold-icon-open { display: none; }
</style>

<script>
document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', function () {

  /* ── 1. Wrap every cell output in a collapsible <details> ──────────────── */
  var outputSels = [
    '.cell-output-stdout',
    '.cell-output-stderr',
    '.cell-output-display'
  ];

  document.querySelectorAll(outputSels.join(',')).forEach(function (el) {
    if (el.closest('.callout') || el.closest('details.out-fold')) return;

    var hasImg = !!el.querySelector('img, svg, canvas');
    var hasTbl = !!el.querySelector('table');
    var label  = hasImg ? '📊  Show plot'
               : hasTbl ? '📋  Show table'
               :           '📄  Show output';

    var det = document.createElement('details');
    det.className = 'out-fold';

    var sum = document.createElement('summary');
    sum.className = 'out-fold-btn';
    sum.innerHTML =
      '<span class="fold-icon fold-icon-closed">▶</span>' +
      '<span class="fold-icon fold-icon-open">▼</span>' +
      label;

    el.parentNode.insertBefore(det, el);
    det.appendChild(sum);
    det.appendChild(el);
  });

  /* ── 2. Append the floating control bar to <body> ──────────────────────── */
  var bar = document.createElement('div');
  bar.id = 'global-output-toggle';
  bar.innerHTML =
    '<span>Quick controls:</span>' +
    '<button id="code-toggle-btn"   class="gtbtn code-btn"   onclick="toggleCode()">▼ Expand All Code</button>' +
    '<button id="output-toggle-btn" class="gtbtn output-btn" onclick="toggleOutput()">▼ Expand All Output</button>';

  document.body.appendChild(bar);
});

/* Track open/collapsed state separately for code and output */
var codeOpen   = false;
var outputOpen = false;

function toggleCode() {
  codeOpen = !codeOpen;
  document.querySelectorAll('details').forEach(function (d) {
    /* Code details contain a .sourceCode descendant; output folds carry class out-fold */
    if (!d.classList.contains('out-fold') && d.querySelector('.sourceCode')) {
      d.open = codeOpen;
    }
  });
  var btn = document.getElementById('code-toggle-btn');
  if (btn) btn.textContent = codeOpen ? '▲ Collapse All Code' : '▼ Expand All Code';
}

function toggleOutput() {
  outputOpen = !outputOpen;
  document.querySelectorAll('details.out-fold').forEach(function (d) {
    d.open = outputOpen;
  });
  var btn = document.getElementById('output-toggle-btn');
  if (btn) btn.textContent = outputOpen ? '▲ Collapse All Output' : '▼ Expand All Output';
}
</script>
```

## What This Tutorial Is About

There is a single idea running through everything in this tutorial:

> **Y is supposed to reflect X — but it actually reflects X *plus something else*.**

This problem shows up in five different guises that marketing researchers encounter regularly:

- **Case 1 — Discriminant Validity:** Your Green Purchase Intention scale is supposed to measure *purchase intention* (X) — but its items also pick up *environmental concern* (something else). The scale cannot be told apart from a different construct.

- **Case 2 — Omitted Variable Bias:** In your secondary data, observed *sales* (Y) is supposed to reflect *advertising spend* (X) — but sales also reflects *store quality* (something else, which you never measured). Your estimated advertising effect is inflated.

- **Case 3 — Measurement Non-Invariance:** Your GPI items are supposed to reflect *green purchase intention* (X) across both experimental conditions — but two of the items shift upward in the treatment condition for reasons unrelated to the latent construct. The items don't mean the same thing in both groups.

- **Case 4 — Latent Subgroups:** Your sample contains two very different types of consumers — Green Champions and Price Skeptics — who respond to green marketing completely differently. When you aggregate across both groups, your outcome variable reflects a mixture of two different data-generating processes. The treatment effect you estimate doesn't apply cleanly to anyone.

- **Case 5 — Outliers:** Your dataset contains a cluster of luxury flagship stores that operate on completely different economics from regular stores. Including them distorts every coefficient in your model. Collective outliers are a version of the latent-subgroup problem; point outliers are a different statistical challenge. Both can be handled without deleting data.

The statistical symptoms differ across these five cases, but the **underlying logic is identical**: your outcome variable is contaminated by variance that belongs to something other than the construct or cause you are trying to study.

::: {.callout-note}
## Learning Objectives
By the end of this tutorial, you will be able to:

- Diagnose discriminant validity failures using HTMT and the DVI method (Pieters et al., 2025)
- Identify omitted variable bias through residual diagnostics and coefficient sensitivity analysis
- Test measurement invariance across groups using a configural → metric → scalar sequence in lavaan
- Use modification indices to pinpoint which specific items are non-invariant
- Use Gaussian mixture models (via `mclust`) to detect latent subgroups that distort your outcome
- Apply Local Outlier Factor (LOF) and DBSCAN to identify collective and point outliers
- Apply robust regression (MASS::rlm) as an alternative to deleting outliers
- Explain the conceptual link between all five problems to a non-technical audience
:::


---


## Packages Needed

Install and load the required packages. If you haven't installed them before, run the `install.packages()` lines first.

```{r packages}
#| code-summary: "▶ Load required packages"
# Uncomment to install if needed:
# install.packages(c("lavaan", "semTools", "MASS", "ggplot2",
#                    "dplyr", "tidyr", "corrplot", "knitr", "lmtest",
#                    "mclust", "dbscan"))

library(lavaan)       # CFA and SEM (Cases 1 and 3)
library(semTools)     # htmt() and auxiliary SEM tools (Cases 1 and 3)
library(MASS)         # mvrnorm(): generate multivariate normal data (all cases)
library(ggplot2)      # Visualizations (all cases)
library(dplyr)        # Data manipulation (all cases)
library(tidyr)        # Data reshaping (Cases 2 and 3)
library(corrplot)     # Correlation heatmap (Case 1)
library(knitr)        # Nicely formatted tables (all cases)
library(lmtest)       # Breusch-Pagan heteroskedasticity test (Case 2)
library(mclust)       # Gaussian mixture models / latent class analysis (Case 4)
library(dbscan)       # Local outlier factor and DBSCAN clustering (Case 5)
```


---
